key_strengths: Mission-critical recurring payroll/HCM service for ~1.1M employers
Switching-cost + regulatory-scale moat in enterprise and mid-market
51 consecutive years of dividend increases; AA- credit rating
26.3% operating margin, 21.4% FCF margin, 108% FCF conversion
Owner earnings ($4.1B) essentially equal reported net income
Trading below 10-year trough P/E on FY26 >6% revenue / 9-10% EPS guide
key_risks: US private employment contraction pressuring pays-per-control and bookings
Float income rate-sensitive; Fed cuts compress ~$1.3B interest stream
SMB share loss to Rippling, Gusto, Deel, Paycom
Segment-level margin compression (ES -50 bps, PEO -70 bps in Q2 FY26)
FY25 total debt tripled $3.3B to $9.1B - composition not fully confirmed
10-month drawdown with no durable bottom - momentum unfavorable
red_flags: Client revenue retention guided down 10-30 bps in FY26 - first negative move on moat metric
CEO comp up 20.6% y/y to $19.1M while stock underperformed
Buybacks steady-dollar, not opportunistic at today's discount
No visible insider open-market buying cluster during 37% drawdown
Price MOS vs bear IV is -37% - margin of safety in business quality, not price
buy_triggers: Two consecutive quarters of >6% Employer Services bookings growth with stable/rising retention
Monthly ADP private payroll prints >75k for three months running
Additional 10% price decline with thesis intact - scale starter 3% to 5%
sell_triggers: Annual client revenue retention below 88% for two consecutive years
Forward P/E above 28x approaching 10-year peak without earnings acceleration
Identification of higher-conviction Own-verdict name with MC P(IV > Price) > 90%
valuation_summary: Quant DCF bear/base/bull IV of $146/$228/$322 vs current price $200.47 - price sits ~12% below base IV with Monte Carlo 86.8% probability of undervaluation, but negative -37% strict MOS vs bear IV means safety is in business quality (AA- credit, 99% recurring, 92% retention) rather than deep price discount.
compact_checklist: Business: ADP runs payroll, HR, benefits, tax filing and PEO services for ~1.1 million employers across ~140 countries, earning recurring per-employee fees plus interest on ~$30B+ of client funds held in transit - the payroll backbone for roughly one in six US workers.

Moat: Switching costs (6-12 month mid-market migrations, 91-92% client revenue retention) plus 75 years of multi-jurisdiction tax-compliance infrastructure and scale in bank/agency plumbing keep enterprise and mid-market clients from defecting - even when Rippling and Gusto chip away at the SMB flank.

Economics: Operating margin expanded from 23.1% (FY22) to 26.3% (FY25), FY25 FCF was $4.4B at 21.4% margin and 108% conversion, reported ROIC runs 37-54%, and owner earnings of $4.1B essentially equal GAAP net income - the financial fingerprint of a genuine moat compounder.

Management: CEO Maria Black (26-year insider) runs a company with 51 consecutive years of dividend increases, modest SBC (1.3% of revenue), clean governance, and $1.1-2.0B/yr of buybacks - competent caretaker-quality but not opportunistic (FY25 buybacks were near base IV, not at the current drawdown prices).

Debt risk: S&P AA-, net debt/EBITDA 0.9x, interest coverage 12.6x, $7.8B cash, and ~99% recurring revenue - even a 30% two-year revenue drop leaves ADP comfortably profitable and dividend-capable, so survival is not in question.

Price: At $200.47 the market implicitly prices ~3% long-term growth at ~10% cost of equity on peak EPS - which requires either a sustained US employment contraction or that current earnings are cyclically inflated by float income that will fade hard; both premises are at the pessimistic end of reasonable.

Margin of safety: Price sits 37% ABOVE quant bear IV ($146) so price MOS is strictly negative, but Monte Carlo P(IV > Price) is 86.8%, 19 of 25 sensitivity cells exceed spot, and the real safety is in the business itself (AA- balance sheet, 92% retention, essential service) - not a deep-discount setup, a quality-at-a-fair-price setup.

Thesis breaker: Annual client revenue retention falling below 88% for two consecutive years, or FY bookings growth sustained below 3%, would confirm that the switching-cost moat is actually eroding and trigger an exit.
source_summary: yfinance financials.md/json (2026-04-19), src/quant deterministic DCF (2026-04-18), ADP Q2 FY26 earnings release and call (Jan 2026), WebSearch for analyst targets / peer multiples / historical P/E / S&P credit ratings / competitor context; no live 10-K read, so FY25 short-term debt composition and CEO proxy comp detail relied on secondary summaries that cross-agreed.
